---
title: "MESA: Mapping Epidemiology in Sparse Areas"
subtitle: "A desktop tool for public-health spatial analysis where data is thin"
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Spatial statistics]{.tag} [C++]{.tag} [Desktop software]{.tag} [Geocoding]{.tag} [Public health]{.tag}
:::

## Problem

Most spatial-epidemiology tooling assumes you have plenty of data and a GIS
analyst to drive it. Rural and frontier health jurisdictions usually have
neither. They have few cases, large and sparsely populated areas, and staff who
need an answer without writing code. Standard hot-spot methods can behave badly
with small counts and irregular geography, and the good implementations live in
libraries that assume a Python or R workflow.

MESA puts credible spatial-cluster analysis in front of people doing sparse-area
public health, as a desktop application they can run themselves.

## Approach

It's a self-contained C++ desktop app. It takes addresses or points, geocodes
them, and runs local spatial statistics to find clusters. The statistics are
implemented carefully and checked against reference libraries, so the results
hold up instead of just looking plausible. The goal is to be correct and to run
offline, not to be a general-purpose GIS.

## Methods and tools

- **Geocoding.** Turn address or place data into coordinates for analysis.
- **Spatial cluster detection.** Local indicators of spatial association:
  Getis-Ord Gi\* for hot- and cold-spot detection, Moran's I for spatial
  autocorrelation.
- **Significance testing.** Monte Carlo / permutation testing for the local
  statistics, so significance reflects the data rather than an asymptotic
  assumption that may not hold at small counts.
- **Validation.** Output is checked against established implementations (PySAL
  and R spatial packages) so results line up with known-good tools before I
  trust the app.
- **Stack.** C++ desktop application, built to run locally without a server or
  cloud dependency.

## Status

Active personal project. The core spatial statistics and their validation are
the part I care most about getting right; the application around them is the
delivery mechanism.

## What this demonstrates

- Implementing spatial statistics (Gi\*, Moran's I, permutation inference) from
  the ground up and validating them against reference libraries.
- Building fast, self-contained desktop software in C++.
- Building a public-health tool for a specific, underserved use case.
